content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Joe Naiman 
5162 College Gardens ct. 
San Diego, CA 
92115 
Dear Mr. Naiman: 
June 23, 1989 
Re: 
Letter No. G-89-322 
Enclosed for your information are FPPC Forms 501 and 502 with 
instructions for completing them and a campaign disclosure manual. 
These forms must be filed with the Commission, either in person or 
by mail, before you solicit or receive any contribution for 
elective office. 
Since your letter asks other questions which are governed by 
the Elections Code, we are referring it back to the Secretary of 
State's office. 
The Commission has no jurisdiction to interpret 
the Elections Code. 
(Government Code Section 83111.) 
Sincerely, 
{:;~r/""--' ~c\ v~· f ~t, .. "-~~V 
'.J 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
KED:plh 
Enclosures 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 9S804~0807 • (916) 322~5660 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Deborah Seiler 
Secretary of State's Office 
1230 J street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Ms. Seiler: 
June 23, 1989 
Enclosed is a letter from Mr. Joe Naiman that was referred to 
us by your office. 
We have responded to Mr. Naiman's question 
concerning the Political Reform Act. 
We are returning the letter 
to you since it contains other questions governed by the Elections 
Code. 
For your information, a copy of our response to Mr. Naiman 
also is enclosed. 
KED:plh 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
P~t1-.A..',v-" f 
,) 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • 
P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804,0807 • (916)322,5660 


Hon. March Fong Eu 
Secretary of State 
1230 J Street 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Dear Secretary Eu: 
Pli '89 
5162 College Gardens Ct. 
San Diego, CA 92115 
May 17, 1989 
I am interested in running for the office of Insurance Commissioner in 
next year's election, and would like some information regarding campaigning 
for that post. 
1) 
Do I become an announced candidate merely by stating my intention to 
run, or need I file candidate declaration forms? 
2) 
Must I go to Sacramento to file, or may I receive the filing forms by 
mail and return them to your office? 
3) 
Having views that go along no party line, I would run either as an 
independent or start my own party. 
3a) 
If I were to run as an independent, how many signatures would I 
need to file (or to avoid the filing fee), and must I get them from those who 
are registered "declined to state" or may I obtain these signatures from any 
registered voter who has not signed the petition of another candidate? 
3b) 
If I were to start my own party, how many voters must re-register 
into this party for it to obtain ballot status in 1990, by what date must this 
minimum be reached, and how many signatures would be needed? 
I look forward to your response. 
cooperation. 
Sincerely, 
~~ 
Joe Naiman 
Thank you for your time and 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Joe Naiman 
5162 College Gardens ct. 
San Diego, CA 
92115 
Dear Mr. Naiman: 
May 25, 1989 
Re: 
Letter No. 89-322 
Your letter requesting advice under the political Reform Act 
was received on May 24, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices 
Commission. 
If you have any questions about your advice request, 
you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or 
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21 
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. 
If more 
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response 
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to the 
information needed. 
If your request is for informal assistance, 
we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See commission 
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
V;{y truly YOu _

sections.conclusion: These forms must be filed with the Commission, either in person or by mail, before you solicit or receive any contribution for elective office. Since your letter asks other questions which are governed by the Elections Code, we are referring it back to the Secretary of State's office. The Commission has no jurisdiction to interpret the Elections Code. (Government Code Section 83111.)
sections.question_synthetic: Mr. Naiman inquired about candidate declaration requirements, filing procedures, and petition signature requirements for running as an independent or starting a new political party for the 1990 Insurance Commissioner election.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC addressed only questions falling under the Political Reform Act regarding campaign contribution disclosure forms (Forms 501 and 502), and referred all Elections Code questions back to the Secretary of State's office as outside FPPC jurisdiction.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of four related letters (initial request, FPPC acknowledgment, FPPC response, and inter-agency referral). No formal Q&A section headers present but questions are clearly stated in initial letter. Main FPPC response indicates partial jurisdiction; some questions fall under Political Reform Act (FPPC jurisdiction) while others fall under Elections Code (Secretary of State jurisdiction).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Mr. Naiman inquired about candidate declaration requirements, filing procedures, and petition signature requirements for running as an independent or starting a new political party for the 1990 Insurance Commissioner election.

CONCLUSION: These forms must be filed with the Commission, either in person or by mail, before you solicit or receive any contribution for elective office. Since your letter asks other questions which are governed by the Elections Code, we are referring it back to the Secretary of State's office. The Commission has no jurisdiction to interpret the Elections Code. (Government Code Section 83111.)

embedding.summary: This document package consists of Mr. Naiman's initial letter to the Secretary of State requesting information about running for Insurance Commissioner, the FPPC's acknowledgment of his advice request, the FPPC's response letter providing Forms 501 and 502 but referring Elections Code questions to the Secretary of State, and the FPPC's letter to the Secretary of State returning the referred questions.
